text: Why partner with MJS: three reasons customers stay > Three reasons customers stay. When customers ask why they should work with us, the answer comes down to three things. First, depth of materials expertise. Our packaging engineers have spent decades inside food, pharmaceutical and cosmetics supply chains, and that experience shows up in fewer line stoppages and faster qualification.

Why partner with MJS: three reasons customers stay > Three reasons customers stay. Second, an integrated supply approach. We do not just ship containers; we co-design closures, label substrates, and secondary packaging together, so the line works the first time. Third, the relationship outlasts the project. Most of our largest accounts have been with MJS for more than a decade, and that continuity means the team you start with is the team you scale with.

Case study: pharma-grade glass vials for an injectable launch > The brief. A clinical-stage biotech needed Type I borosilicate vials that could pass USP 660 chemical resistance and hold a sensitive monoclonal antibody at minus eighty Celsius.

Case study: pharma-grade glass vials for an injectable launch > Qualification. We qualified two MJS pharma-grade glass vial SKUs against their fill-finish line, built a custom siliconisation profile for the closures, and supported their regulatory submission with a full extractables and leachables package. The launch shipped on time, with zero deviations attributable to primary packaging.

Product: borosilicate Type I pharma-grade glass vials > Overview. MJS borosilicate Type I vials are manufactured to USP 660 standards in fill volumes from two to one hundred millilitres. Each lot is supplied with certificates of analysis covering hydrolytic resistance, dimensional tolerances, and particulate burden.

Product: borosilicate Type I pharma-grade glass vials > Specification. property: hydrolytic_resistance, value: USP <660> Type I. property: dimensional_tolerance, value: +/- 0.10 mm OD. property: particulate_burden, value: <= 6000 particles >= 10um per container. Compatible closures are listed in the product datasheet. Lead time on stock SKUs is three weeks; custom geometries are eight to twelve weeks depending on annealing schedule.
Welcome to episode 21. Today I am joined by Dr Lena Weiss, who runs cold-chain qualification at a top-five biopharma. Lena, welcome.

Why borosilicate Type I is still the default for biologics. The honest answer is that the alternatives still trade at a quality risk we are not willing to take for an injectable program at minus eighty. Glass is well-characterised, the supply chain is mature, and the failure modes are known.

What fails first when a vial is cycled too aggressively. The closure usually moves before the glass does. You will see torque drift, then liner deformation, and only then any glass effect. Designing the stability protocol to catch closure movement at the first time-point is what separates a clean qualification from a noisy one.

Welcome to the MJS pharma-grade glass production line. I am the plant manager and over the next ten minutes I am going to walk you through the four stations that matter most for primary packaging quality.

Annealing oven calibration. We run a controlled cooling profile after forming, and the calibration of this oven directly drives the residual stress in the finished vial. We re-baseline weekly against a thermocouple reference array.
Inline particle inspection. Every vial passes the camera array you can see here. The threshold is set against the USP 788 particulate limits, with a margin so we never ship a borderline lot.
